summary: Occupy the SEC, a working group from the Occupy movement, successfully influenced the final Volcker Rule by submitting a detailed public comment letter that was cited 284 times in the rule's preamble. Despite giving the final rule only a C- grade and facing obstacles like being denied standing when they sued regulators for delays, the group demonstrated that citizen engagement in regulatory processes can work. The author witnessed their methodical, workmanlike approach—meeting after hours to review the proposed rule line by line—and contrasts their grassroots effort with the extensive lobbyist access to rule-writers. The post celebrates public commenting as a critical democratic tool while acknowledging it requires both committed citizens and receptive regulators.
key_points: Occupy the SEC's public comment letter was referenced 284 times in the Volcker Rule's final preamble, demonstrating measurable impact
The group worked methodically through the proposed rule line by line in after-hours meetings, prioritizing substance over rhetoric
Public commenting is a powerful but underutilized tool for citizen participation in regulatory processes
Success required both dedicated citizen effort and regulators willing to listen—the combination is rare but possible
The group graded the final rule a C-, showing they maintained critical standards even while celebrating their influence
money_quotes: It was a great example of an Occupy group that was all work and no talk.
I even remember talking to them about the chances anybody would even read their letter, never mind take it seriously.
They knew the rules, though, and the rules were that the regulators would have to read all the letters, although how seriously they took them was a different matter.
They were doing it because they knew it was one of the best shots at having their voices heard. It was an incredible commitment to that ideal.
Nothing could have been more workmanlike or hopeful.
Public commenting is a critical tool.
key_insight: Effective activism sometimes means mastering bureaucratic processes rather than just protesting—Occupy the SEC showed that citizens who do the unglamorous work of reading regulations line by line can actually influence policy.
